pattern: Using every ticket exactly once is an Eulerian path, and processing each airport's destinations in sorted order while building the route on the way back out of a depth-first search yields the lexicographically smallest valid itinerary.
hints: Every ticket must be used exactly once, so you are looking for a trail that traverses every edge of a directed multigraph, always starting at the same fixed airport.
Sort each airport's outgoing destinations so that, when you have a choice, you always try the alphabetically smaller one first.
Run a depth-first walk that consumes edges as it goes and prepends each airport to the answer only when it has no remaining outgoing tickets, then reverse the collected list.

edge_inputs.description: a chain that forces a single ordering
two choices from JFK where the greedy smaller-first pick would dead-end early
duplicate identical tickets between the same pair
a self-loop-free graph where lexicographic order conflicts with completing the trail
all tickets leave and return to JFK forming a star

stress.description: random Eulerian graphs built by walking a closed loop back to JFK so a valid itinerary always exists
stress.generator_python:
def gen(rng, size):
    m = max(1, min(size, 280))
    codes = ['JFK']
    for i in range(1, 14):
        codes.append('A' + str(i).zfill(2))
    tickets = []
    cur = 'JFK'
    for _ in range(m - 1):
        nxt = rng.choice(codes)
        tickets.append([cur, nxt])
        cur = nxt
    tickets.append([cur, 'JFK'])
    rng.shuffle(tickets)
    return [tickets]
stress.note: closing the walk back to JFK guarantees an Eulerian trail exists so the reference never dead-ends
